turns.user_message: I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
step2_rag_chunks: Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. Making a daily plan for managing cravings can make the quitting process easier.

Patient: Maybe I could tell them I'm trying to quit, that way they'll leave me alone. But then I'd feel embarrassed if I end up smoking anyway. It's easier just to give in and have one, yaar.
Therapist: You're feeling really torn between wanting to quit and feeling like you'll let your coworkers and yourself down if you tell them you're trying to quit.
Can you imagine how it would feel to have someone you trust tell you they're quitting smoking and ask for your support, rather than feeling like you're asking for their help?
Patient: I guess it would be nice to have someone believe in me enough to think I can quit. My doctor said I had to come here, so that means he thinks I can do it too, right?

Therapist: You're feeling a sense of validation and support from your doctor believing in you to quit smoking. That's a great start. What do you think you would say to your doctor if he asked you how you're doing with quitting so far?

Patient: Honestly? I'm not sure. I'm still smoking, but...I don't know, it feels different now. Talking about it here has made me realize I don't want to be a slave to my coworkers and my stress. Maybe I can try to cut back or something.

step2_rag_chunks: Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready.
